coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots with nitrogen-doped content dependence for highly efficient dual-modality photodynamic antimicrobial therapy and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Kuo, Wen-Shuo
Chen, Hua-Han
Chen, Shih-Yao
Chang, Chia-Yuan
Chen, Pei-Chi
Hou, Yung-I
Shao, Yu-Ting
Kao, Hui-Fang
Lilian Hsu, Chih-Li
Chen, Yi-Chun
Chen, Shean-Jen
Wu, Shang-Rung
Wang, Jiu-Yao
coredata.dc:description: Reactive oxygen species is the main contributor to photodynamic therapy. The results of this study show that a nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot, serving as a photosensitizer, was capable of generating a higher amount of reactive oxygen species than a nitrogen-free graphene quantum dot in photodynamic therapy when photoexcited for only 3 min of 670 nm laser exposure (0.1 W cm-2), indicating highly improved antimicrobial effects. In addition, we found that higher nitrogen-bonding compositions of graphene quantum dots more efficiently performed photodynamic therapy actions than did the lower compositions that underwent identical treatments. Furthermore, the intrinsically emitted luminescence from nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and high photostability simultaneously enabled it to act as a promising contrast probe for tracking and localizing bacteria in biomedical imaging. Thus, the dual modality of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots presents possibilities for future clinical applications, and in particular multidrug resistant bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reactive oxygen species
Photodynamic therapy
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot
Antimicrobial
Photostability
Contrast probe

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Biomaterials at the Nanoscale for Diagnostic Systems

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biocompatibility assay with colony forming unit (CFU) counting method
Determining bacteria viability rates after laser exposure
Temperature dependence of exposure time for nanomaterials using 670-nm laser exposure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(a) High-resolution TEM image of single N-GQD, illustrating the graphene
{
1
1 ¯
00
}
lattice planes with a d-spacing of 0.213 nm and showing the histogram of size distribution by DLS (inset: low-magnification TEM image of N-GQD). (b) The calculated interlayer distance of N-GQD was 0.360 nm by the XRD pattern. (c) UV–vis spectrum of N-GQD.

The surface chemistry of N-GQD (5.1%) was determined by XPS. (a) The deconvoluted C(1s) XPS spectra and fitted peaks using Gaussian function,: CC/CC, CN, CO, CO, and OCO; (b) N(1s) spectra and fitted peaks of XPS using Gaussian function,: pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and quaternary N.

Measurements to directly detect singlet oxygen (1O2) by (a) SOSG reagent and (b) t-MVP, and (c) superoxide radical anion (O2
−) by XTT with 1–3 min 670 nm laser irradiated to N-GQDs (5.1%) were done. Delivered dose: 0–10 μg mL−1 N-GQD (5.1%). ROS neutralization: with the treatments of nanomaterial, a 3-min laser irradiation and 30 ppm of antioxidant α-Tocopherol/methyl linoleate. Positive control: the treatments of 50 μM tert-butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP) [30] and a 3-min laser irradiation. Data are means ± SD (n = 5).

Viability (%) was quantified following the determined viable count of nanomaterials-AbLPS-treated-E. coli via CFU assay with a 670 nm laser for a 3-min exposure. Delivered dose: OD600 ∼ 0.05 of E. coli and 1 μg mL−1 nanomaterial-AbLPS. Data are means ± SD (n = 5).

Detection of singlet oxygen (1O2) by (a) SOSG reagent and (b) t-MVP, and (c) superoxide radical anion (O2
−) by XTT with 3 min 670 nm laser irradiated to N-GQD (5.1%)-, N-GQD (2.9%)- and GQD-AbLPS-treated-E. coli were done. Delivered dose: OD600 ∼ 0.05 of E. coli and 1 μg mL−1 nanomaterial-AbLPS). Negative control: bacteria and laser irradiation alone without nanomaterial treatment (0 μg mL−1). ROS neutralization: with the treatments of 1 μg mL−1 nanomaterial-AbLPS-treated-bacteria, laser irradiation and 30 ppm of antioxidant α-Tocopherol/methyl linoleate. Positive control: with the treatments of 50 μM TBHP and a 3-min laser irradiation. Data are means ± SD (n = 5).

TEM images. (a) Showing bare E. coli without any treatment. Bacteria treated with N-GQD (5.1%)-AbLPS for (b) 3 h and (c) 5 d of incubation, respectively. (d) The photoexcited nanomaterial-treated bacteria (3 h of incubation) with a 670 nm laser exposure for 3 min. Delivered dose: OD600 ∼ 0.05 of E. coli and 1 μg mL−1 N-GQD (5.1%)-AbLPS.

Luminescence images (gray level) of (a) bare E. coli without any treatment, and (b,c) N-GQD (5.1%)-AbLPS-treated-E. coli for 3 h of incubation and photoexcited nanomaterial-treated bacteria with a 670 nm laser exposure from 0 to 3 min. Delivered dose: OD600 ∼ 0.05 of E. coli and 1 μg mL−1 N-GQD (5.1%)-AbLPS.

Images and viability of E. coli. (a,b) After the 3-min 670 nm laser photoexcitation of N-GQD (5.1%)-AbLPS-treated-bacteria, Live/Dead kit was used to stain bacteria and the images were obtained, and (c) quantification of viability was determined. Delivered dose: OD600 ∼ 0.05 of E. coli and 1 μg mL−1 N-GQD (5.1%)-AbLPS. Negative control: bacteria and laser irradiation alone without nanomaterial treatment. Data are means ± SD (n = 5). *p value obtained by Student's t-test (p < 0.01).
O(1s)/C(1s) and N(1s)/C(1s) atomic ratios of N-GQD, C and N bonding composition examined by XPS.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots with nitrogen-doped content dependence for highly efficient dual-modality photodynamic antimicrobial therapy and bioimaging

Corresponding author. Institute of Basic Medical Sciences, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 701, Taiwan, ROC.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reactive oxygen species is the main contributor to photodynamic therapy. The results of this study show that a nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot, serving as a photosensitizer, was capable of generating a higher amount of reactive oxygen species than a nitrogen-free graphene quantum dot in photodynamic therapy when photoexcited for only 3 min of 670 nm laser exposure (0.1 W cm-2), indicating highly improved antimicrobial effects. In addition, we found that higher nitrogen-bonding compositions of graphene quantum dots more efficiently performed photodynamic therapy actions than did the lower compositions that underwent identical treatments. Furthermore, the intrinsically emitted luminescence from nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and high photostability simultaneously enabled it to act as a promising contrast probe for tracking and localizing bacteria in biomedical imaging. Thus, the dual modality of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots presents possibilities for future clinical applications, and in particular multidrug resistant bacteria.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a type of phototherapy that has established medical applications, involving selectively exposing photosensitizers (PSs) to light of appropriate wavelength and energy. In addition to conventional PSs [1,2], several newly synthesized PSs [3,4] have recently been prepared and applied. With recent developments in nanotechnology, the use of nanomaterials in PDT has served as a potential alternative approach to achieving improved therapeutic efficacy [5,6]. Graphene-based nanomaterial has shown potential in the pursuit of applying new nanomaterials to efficiently perform PDT. Graphene is a two-dimensional monolayer of graphite that bonds carbon (C) atoms, forming a hexagonal lattice. Graphene-based materials have been investigated in numerous fields; they exhibit excellent conductivity as well as mechanical, thermal, and optical properties. Although some studies have reported using graphene-based nanomaterials combined with PSs for PDT [7,8], research on the direct use of a graphene quantum dot (GQD) as a PS for generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) in developing PDT has been limited [9–12]. The GQD is a new type of zero-dimensional QD converted from two-dimensional graphene sheets that is emerging as a promising optical nanomaterial; contributions from intrinsic- and defective-state emission, possibly involving photoluminescence (PL) mechanisms and sequential GQDs, could be applied to biomedical research, including PDT research. Furthermore, doping is crucial in semiconductors because the carrier density can be noticeably changed, resulting in optical and electrical properties completely distinct from the intrinsic counterpart. Consequently, nitrogen (N) functionalization and doping is considered to be highly useful in modifying the intrinsic properties of GQDs because the five-valance electrons and incorporated electron-donating N atoms, which have a comparable atomic size to that of C atoms, impart a relatively high positive-charge density to adjacent C atoms in graphene [13]. Thus, given GQDs exhibiting extraordinary quantum confinement and edge effects, N atoms doped with GQDs (N-GQDs) can exhibit enhanced electrocatalytic, electrochemical, and photochemical activities, providing advantages in biomedical and optoelectronic applications [14].

This study employed N-GQDs as PSs to eliminate the gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) by using 670-nm laser exposure involving a PDT pathway. A limited quantity (1 μg mL−1) of N-GQDs with an N 1s–C 1s ratio of approximately 5.1%, determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), with a 3-min photoexcitation time and low-dose energy irradiation (0.1 W cm−2) led to 100% bacterial elimination, compared with approximately 52% elimination when conducting the same treatment with a GQD. Additionally, in contrast to a lower N content (2.9%), a higher N content in N-GQDs (5.1%) enhanced PDT efficacy and achieved improved bactericidal capability. Furthermore, the photoproperty of emitted PL (Em: 728 nm) in a near-infrared (NIR) region, employing slight scattering, low energy absorption, and optimal irradiation penetration, enabled N-GQDs to serve in this study as promising contrast probes to track and localize N-GQD-treated-bacteria and provide additional data on the irradiated bacterial status. The integration of N-GQDs, dual-modality PDT, and a contrast agent was found to have high potential in simultaneous PDT for eliminating bacteria and tracking targeted bacteria to evaluate the therapeutic effect.

Graphene oxide was prepared from the graphite (Bay carbon, SP-1, USA) with a modified Hummers method [15]. Graphite (8.5 M) and NaNO3 (0.6 M) (Merck Germany) were mixed with H2SO4 (18 M) (Wako, Japan). KMnO4 (2.0 M) (Merck Germany) was slowly added and kept stirring overnight. Then, the ddH2O was gradually added and kept stirred. Adding H2O2 (35 wt %) (Shimakyu, Japan) to terminate reaction. Washing and centrifugation with ddH2O several times were addressed and the graphene oxide was collected. The as-prepared graphene oxide was heated to 400–600 °C (400 °C and 600 °C for N-GQD 2.9% and 5.1% of the N(1)/C(1s) ratio for N-GQD determined by XPS, respectively) in the presence of ammonia for several hours, and then were introduced to concentrated HNO3 (16 M) (Wako, Japan) and stirred overnight. The mixture was put in sonicator and then put it in oven at 160 °C to vaporize all the liquid. Washing and centrifugation (83000 rpm) (Optima TLX Ultracentrifuge, BECKMAN, USA) with ddH2O several times were addressed. The resulting black suspension was tuned the pH to 7.4 with NaOH (Merck Germany), and it was stayed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 100 kDa) > 12 h, and N-GQD was obtained. On the other hand, the as-prepared graphene oxide was put in a tube furnace to elevate the temperature to 400 °C for several hours in the presence of argon and then conducted the same treatment for the following steps to prepare GQD.

The samples were then subject to transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (JEOL 1400 and JEOL 2100F, Japan) observation. The height profile diagram, thickness and size of samples were determined by atomic force microscopy (AFM, multimode 8, Bruker, Germany). The crystalline structures of samples were identified using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker AXS Gmbh, Germany/D2 Phaser) with Cuκα radiation at 30 kv and 30 mA. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis), zeta potential spectra and dynamic light scattering (DLS) of samples were recorded by the spectrometers: PerkinElmer RX1 USA, U-4100 Hitachi Japan and Malvern Nano-ZS90 UK, respectively. Raman spectroscopy (DXR, Thermo Scientific, USA) was examined the crystallinity of samples with 532 nm laser. XPS (PHI 5000, VersaProbe, USA) was examined the surface chemistry of materials. The PL signal was recorded by the spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan).

E. coli, obtained from our own laboratory were grown on the nutrient agar plate of LB, composed of agar 15 g, sodium chloride 8 g, yeast extract 5 g, tryptone 10 g for per liter and tune pH to 7.5) (Sigma Aldrich Co., USA) and incubated at 37 °C.

The absorbance of a quantity of antibody (anti-lipopolysaccharide (LPS) antibody (AbLPS) or anti-epidermal growth factor receptor antibody (AbEGFR) (Antagene, USA)) was recorded by UV–vis spectroscopy (Abs: approximately 276 nm). By the electrostatic interaction, the nanomaterials were mixed with the same quantity antibody for 30 min of incubation at 4 °C in the dark and centrifuge (83000 rpm) to remove excess antibody, and then the nanomaterial-AbLPS, or -AbEGFR was prepared. On the other hand, keep the supernatant and measure its absorbance. The difference in absorbance between the collected supernatant and the original antibody was estimated. Consequentially, the quantity of the antibody absorbed on the nanomaterials was calculated by Lambert-Beer's law. There was approximately 8.5 μg of AbLPS absorbed on 0.1 mg of N-GQDs (5.1%), which meant the coating efficiency was approximately 8.5%, whereas 11.7% for AbEGFR. For N-GQDs (2.9%), the coating efficiency was 8.2% for AbLPS and 11.7% for AbEGFR, whereas 8.7% of AbLPS and 12.1% of AbEGFR, absorbed with GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility assay with colony forming unit (CFU) counting method
ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria (OD600 ∼0.05) were added with nanomaterials-AbLPS (0–10 μg mL−1), by 3 h of incubation at 37 °C. After incubation, the mixture was centrifuged and the pellet of bacteria were diluted (OD600 ∼0.05). Then, a dilution factor of 10−5 to 10−8 was conducted in the incubated bacteria with plating on the plates. The plates were stayed in an incubator at 37 °C overnight. The number of surviving bacteria was determined and expressed as a percentage (%) that corresponds to the unit of CFU mL−1 after incubation.

(a) Nanomaterials-AbLPS (from 1 to 10 μg mL−1) were exposed to a 1–3 min 670 nm laser irradiation (0.1 W cm−2, COHERENT, USA), (and for ROS neutralization: 30 ppm of antioxidant α-Tocopherol (Sigma, USA)/methyl linoleate (Sigma, USA) was added in the dark [16,17]) and then 1 μM of Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green (SOSG) Reagent (Invitrogen, USA) (Ex/Em: 488/525 nm) was added in the dark. On the other hand, nanomaterials-AbLPS or -AbEGFR (from 1 to 10 μg mL−1) was treated with E. coli (OD600 ∼0.05) or human squamous carcinoma cell line (A431 cancer cells), and then incubated for 3 h at 37 °C in the dark. After incubation, the mixture was centrifuged to remove the excess nanomaterials, keep the pellet, and then the bacteria were diluted (OD600 ∼0.05) (cancer cells: remove, replace with new medium and photoexcite directly without dilution), photoexcited with the same treatment, (and for ROS neutralization: α-Tocopherol/methyl linoleate was added in the dark) incubated for 3 h at 37 °C in the dark to proceed PDT action and mixed with SOSG Reagent. Measurements were obtained using a PL spectrometer [18–20]. (b) Nanomaterials-AbLPS (from 1 to 10 μg mL−1) were exposed to a 1–3 min 670 nm laser irradiation (0.1 W cm−2), (and for ROS neutralization: 30 ppm of antioxidant α-Tocopherol/methyl linoleate was added in the dark) and then 10 μM of trans-1-(2′-methoxyvinyl)pyrene (t-MVP) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA)/0.1 M SDS (Sigma, USA) (Ex/Em: 352/465 nm) was added in the dark. On the other hand, nanomaterials-AbLPS or -AbEGFR (from 1 to 10 μg mL−1) was respectively treated with E. coli (OD600 ∼0.05) or A431 cancer cells, and then incubated for 3 h at 37 °C in the dark. After incubation, the mixture was centrifuged to remove the excess nanomaterials, keep the pellet, and then the bacteria were diluted (OD600 ∼0.05) (cancer cells: remove, replace with new medium and photoexcite directly without dilution), photoexcited with the same treatment, (and for ROS neutralization: α-Tocopherol/methyl linoleate was added in the dark) incubated for 3 h at 37 °C in the dark to proceed PDT action and mixed with t-MVP/0.1 M SDS. Measurements were obtained using a PL spectrometer [21].

Nanomaterials-AbLPS (from 1 to 10 μg mL−1) were exposed to a 1–3 min 670 nm laser irradiation (0.1 W cm−2), (and for ROS neutralization: 30 ppm of antioxidant α-Tocopherol/methyl linoleate was added in the dark) and then 2,3-bis (2-methoxy-4-nitro-5-sulfophenyl)- 2H-tetrazolium-5- carboxanilide (XTT) (0.45 mM) (Sigma-Aldrich Co., USA) was added in the dark. XTT would interact with O2
− and produce the XTT-formazan generating strong absorption (470 nm in wavelength) [19], which was monitored with UV–vis spectrometer. On the other hand, nanomaterials-AbLPS or -AbEGFR (from 1 to 10 μg mL−1) was respectively treated with E. coli (OD600 ∼0.05) or A431 cancer cells, and then incubated for 3 h at 37 °C in the dark. After incubation, the mixture was centrifuged to remove the excess nanomaterials, keep the pellet, and then the bacteria were diluted (OD600 ∼0.05) (cancer cells: remove, replace with new medium and photoexcite directly without dilution), photoexcited with the same treatment, (and for ROS neutralization: α-Tocopherol/methyl linoleate was added in the dark) incubated for 3 h at 37 °C in the dark to proceed PDT action and mixed with XTT and recorded the signals with UV–vis spectrometer. Data are means ± SD (n = 5).

According to the previous studies [22,23],ψΔ can be obtained. ψΔ measurements were carried out in D2O at 355 nm, using meso-tetra(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphine dihydrochloride (TSPP) (Frontier Scientific, USA) as a reference (ψΔ = 0.64). TheψΔ of N-GQD (5.1%), N-GQD (2.9%) and GQD was approximately 0.60, 0.49 and 0.41, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determining bacteria viability rates after laser exposure

ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria (OD600 ∼0.05) were added with nanomaterials-AbLPS (1 μg mL−1) to incubate 3 h at 37 °C. After incubation, the mixture was centrifuged and the pellet of bacteria were diluted (OD600 ∼0.05) and exposed to a 3-min 670-nm laser exposure (0.1 W cm−2). Then, a dilution factor of 10−5 to 10−8 was conducted in the incubated bacteria with plating on the agar plates. The plates were stayed in an incubator at 37 °C overnight. The number of surviving bacteria was determined and expressed as a percentage (%) that corresponds to the unit of CFU mL−1 after incubation. Data are means ± SD (n = 5).

Bacteria (OD600 ∼0.05) were added with nanomaterials-AbLPS (1 μg mL−1) to incubate 3 h at 37 °C. After incubation, the mixture was centrifuged and the pellet of bacteria were diluted (OD600 ∼0.05) and exposed to a 3-min 670-nm laser exposure (fixed output power: 0.1 W cm-2), and then SYTO 9/propidium iodide (PI) was treated and mixed. After incubation, the nanomaterials-treated-bacteria were centrifugated for 10 min, after which the pellet was collected. The pellet was resuspended in nutrient medium and PBS, respectively, and centrifuged. Repeat for several time to wash out the nonspecific binding. Then, the pellets were stained by a LIVE (SYTO 9, as displayed with green fluorescence)/DEAD (PI, as displayed with red fluorescence) kit according to the instruction. Images were taken by fluorescence microscopy (OLYMPUS, Japan), and the quantification measurement for live or dead (%) was estimated [18–20]. Data are means ± SD (n = 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature dependence of exposure time for nanomaterials using 670-nm laser exposure
ce:section.ce:para: A thermocouple (Tes Instrument, China) was used to monitor the temperature elevation of nanomaterials (1 μg mL−1) with the treatment of 670-nm laser (0.1 W cm-2) for 0–3 min irradiation times. Data are means ± SD (n = 5).

OD600 ∼ 0.05 of E. coli were incubated with 1 μg mL−1 N-GQD (5.1%)-AbLPS. The absorbance of a quantity of 1 μg mL−1 N-GQD (5.1%)-AbLPS was recorded by UV–vis spectroscopy (Abs: approximately 224 nm). The nanomaterials were mixed with OD600 ∼ 0.05 of E. coli at 37 °C from 1 h to 12 h, centrifuge (1200 rpm) to remove excess nanomaterials, and keep the supernatant and measure its absorbance. The difference in absorbance between the collected supernatant and the original nanomaterials was estimated, leading to the percentage of uptake at each time point.

The relative PL QY of contrast agent is the usually the ratio of the emitted photons to the absorbed photons. It can be led to
QY =
QY
ref
η 2
η
ref
2
I A
A
ref
I
ref
,
where QYref is the QY of Cy5.5 dissolved in DMSO as a reference [24], η is the refractive index of ddH2O = 1.3333 (η ref of DMSO = 1.479), I is the integrated PL intensity and A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength. The absolute PL QY was measured and estimated [25]. The absolute PL QY of N-GQD (5.1%), N-GQD (2.9%) and GQD was approximately 0.259, 0.165 and 0.137, respectively.

The statistical significance of the Live or dead (%) with 1 μg mL−1 of nanomaterials-AbLPS was by the analysis of variance. The p-value < 0.01 was considered statistically significant for all the treatments [26].

N-GQDs were synthesized through an ultrasonic shearing reaction method involving a graphene oxide sheet prepared using a modified Hummers method [15]. Highly magnified HRTEM images presented the mean lateral size of an N-GQD to be approximately 8.0 ± 0.4 nm, further indicating the as-prepared N-GQD interlayer spacing to be 0.213 nm, which corresponded to the d-spacing of the graphene {1
1 ¯
00} lattice planes (Fig. 1 a) [27]. The height profile diagram was determined through AFM, which showed an image of a 1.03 ± 0.02-nm-thick single layer of N-GQDs (SI, Fig. S1). The typical thickness (approximately 0.36 nm) of an N-GQD with an approximately 0.4–0.7-nm increase in graphene thickness was determined by the existence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy groups on two sides of the oxide surface [28]. Fig. 1b shows the XRD results for crystallinity analysis. The N-GQD nanomaterial had a diffraction angle of approximately 2θ = 24.33°, representative of the formation of a well-ordered, favorable layer regularity and lamellar structure with a repeating interlayer distance of 0.360 nm. The distances between the lattice fringes were 0.360 nm and 0.213 nm, consistent with the basal plane distance and the in-plane lattice spacing of graphite, respectively. Additionally, the N-GQD value was consistent with the reported values of 0.340–0.403 nm in other studies [28]. Furthermore, the XRD results suggested no significant basal plane functionalization, consistent with the understanding that graphene has several more active edges than do in-plane Cs, additionally suggesting the existence of functional groups at the edges of the N-GQDs [29]. The increased basal spacing of an N-GQD is related to the accommodation of various oxygen species, such as carboxyl, epoxy, and hydroxyl groups, as well as changes in the C hexahedron grid plane that increase the spacing of the graphene layers. Raman spectroscopy was also employed to examine the crystallinity of the N-GQDs (SI, Fig. S2). According to the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the N-GQDs showed peaks of approximately 224 nm (π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds)—which were red-shifted by approximately 11 nm, in contrast to GQDs of similar size—and 325 nm (n–π* transition of CO shoulder and CN) (Fig. 1c; SI, Fig. S3 and S3d). The absorptions were related to π electron transition in oxygen-containing N-GQDs and indicated N doping in GQDs. The surface chemistry of N-GQDs was determined through XPS. The deconvoluted C 1s N-GQD spectra comprised a nonoxygenated ring (CC/CC, 285.4 eV), CN bonds (286.7 eV), hydroxyl (CO, 287.3 eV), carbonyl (CO, 288.4 eV), and carboxylate (OCO, 289.7 eV) (Fig. 2 a); the deconvoluted N 1s spectra of N-GQDs comprised pyridinic N (398.2 eV), pyrrolic N (399.5 eV), and quaternary N (401.0 eV) (Fig. 2b). The C 1s and N 1s spectra of N-GQD evidenced O 1s–C 1s and N 1s–C 1s ratios of approximately 33.6% and 5.1%, respectively (Table 1 ). FTIR was used to analyze the exposed functional N-GQD groups (SI, Fig. S4a). Because of the exposed carboxylate and hydroxyl groups, the zeta potential results showed the N-GQD surface charge to be approximately −23.0 mV. The results from the characterizations indicated that the N-GQDs were prepared successfully.

ROS induces DNA damage, oxidizes amino acids, and inactivates enzymes; each of these processes lead to bacterial injuries. Singlet oxygen (1O2) and superoxide radical anion (O2
−) of ROS are believed to play major roles in PDT, and the direct detection of N-GQD (5.1%)-produced 1O2 and O2
− through subsequent laser irradiation is necessary. To detect the formation of 1O2 and O2
− in relation to PDT, PDT was generated with the combination of the excited triplet N-GQDs, oxygen and light configured to a suitable wavelength and energy, as well as the introduction of SOSG reagent, t-MVP, and XTT [18,19,21]. The N-GQDs (5.1%) were photoexcited with a 670-nm laser at 1–3 min exposure time and delivered in doses ranging from 1 μg mL−1 to 10 μg mL−1 (Fig. 3 ). To prevent the possibility of 1O2 and O2
− production if the N-GQD (5.1%) was inadvertently exposed to white light, which could compromise the experiment [20], the following PDT-related experiments were also conducted in the dark. The irradiation time for the N-GQDs, dependence of the delivered doses, and quantities of generated 1O2 and O2
− were the crucial data found in this study; N-GQD evidenced applications for PDT concerning its antibacterial effects. To confirm the involvement of ROS in PDT effect of N-GQD (5.1%), the α-Tocopherol was used for the ROS neutralization [16,17]. The amount of the generated ROS was evidently reduced after adding α-Tocopherol and expected the obvious increase in the viability. Unexpectedly, after conducting the same experiment, the lower N-bonding composition of N-GQDs with an approximate ratio of 2.9% N 1–C 1s was determined by XPS (SI, Figs. S5 and S5f, and S5g), indicating a reduced capability to form 1O2 and O2
− compared with N-GQDs with a ratio of 5.1% (SI, Tables S1–S3). Additionally, not only N-GQDs generated more 1O2 and O2
− than did N-free GQDs, but ψΔ of N-GQDs (5.1%), N-GQDs (2.9%) and GQD was measured to be approximately 0.60, 0.49 and 0.41, respectively (ψΔ = 0.64 is the QY of TSPP dissolved in D2O as a reference [22,23]). A summary of characterizations of nanomaterials is shown in Table S4 (SI).

LPS is the major component of the outer membrane of E. coli. In this study, E. coli was selected as the experimental template. To improve its specificity and efficiency, the anti-LPS antibody was coated with materials to form nanomaterial–AbLPS. The number of surviving bacteria was derived according to CFU mL−1 counting assays, which were performed by treating bacteria with N-GQD (5.1%)–AbLPS following E. coli (OD600: 0.05)-treated N-GQD (5.1%)–AbLPS in a range of delivered doses from 1 μg mL−1 to 10 μg mL−1 for 3 h during incubation in the dark at 37 °C. The N-GQDs with a ratio of 5.1% exhibited approximately zero log10 reductions (SI, Fig. S6a), as did the N-GQDs with a ratio of 2.9% and GQDs (SI, Fig. S7), indicating approximately 100% viability (SI, Fig. S6b) and evidencing favorable biocompatibility and exhibited good stability in physiological environment (SI, Table S5). Therefore, a selected 1-μg mL−1 dose of treated nanomaterials was used in the following experiments.

Using TEM to check the images of N-GQD-treated E. coli with laser exposure was necessary. Bare E. coli (Fig. 6 a) after incubation with N-GQD (5.1%)-AbLPS for 3 h (Fig. 6b) resulted in a substantial amount of nanomaterials absorbed onto the bacterial surface because of the highly specific binding ability from the AbLPS antibody. According to the uptake assay, the results also showed that the amount of nanomaterials absorbed onto the bacterial surface was achieved the saturated status in the first 3 h-incubation (SI, Fig. S13). Notably, bacteria must filter external ions and assimilate nutrients to maintain and develop their physiological functions by using the cell wall. Thus, the nanomaterials were absorbed and accumulated to form an external barrier on the bacterial surface. The nanomaterial–AbLPS-treated E. coli demonstrated no exceptional morphology after 3 h incubation, whereas incubation at 5 d radically changed its shape (Fig. 6c), corresponding to nearly 51% viability (SI, Fig. S14), evidencing that the bacteria were unable to function normally; thus, they expired. The absorbed and coated N-GQDs on the bacterial surface caused the suppression of essential nutrients to microbial growth and promoted changes in the properties of membrane wall permeability, provoking internal osmotic imbalances and inhibiting growth of microorganisms, as well as forming an external barrier to suppress microbial growth. Resultantly, the nanomaterials exhibited bacteriostatic or bactericidal capabilities after a long-term incubation of 5 d. By contrast, the photoexcited nanomaterial–AbLPS-treated E. coli (3 h incubation) exhibited drastic damage, resulting in an abnormal morphology (Fig. 6d) that corresponded to nearly 100% death (Fig. 4).

A novel nanomaterial which was able to not only act as a PS but produce high extent of ROS to proceed the alternative approach of PDT involving to eliminate microbes, exclude antibiotics treatments, is currently what we are questing urgently. In this study, results showed high N-bonding composition GQD had the capability to generate more ROS than did the low N-binding GQD by low does of nanomaterial treatment and laser exposure, as well as N-free GQD. In other words, high N-bonding composition GQD conjugated with highly specific antibody performed excellent antimicrobial effect involving PDT action. Besides, the intrinsic luminescence properties of the N-GQD in NIR region and high photostability enable it to be simultaneously exploited as a promising contrast agent to track bacteria in bioimaging. These capabilities could be possibly able to provide an efficient alternative approach for observing and easily eliminating multidrug resistant bacteria in the future. Thus, the dual modality of N-GQD presents possibilities for clinical applications someday.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Ministry of Science Technology (Taiwan) for financially supporting this work (MOST-103-2113-M-006-010-MY2; MOST-103-2321-B-006-030-; MOST-104-2321-B-006-008-; MOST-104-2314-B-006-076-MY3; MOST-105-2627-M-006-002; MOST-105-2320-B-006-017-MY3).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2016.12.022.
